back_translation: All the people of Judah took Uzziah, who was sixteen years old, and made him king in place of his father Amaziah.
He was the one who rebuilt Eloth and restored it to Judah, after King Amaziah rested with his fathers.
Uzziah was sixteen years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem fifty-two years. His mother’s name was Jecoliah of Jerusalem.
He did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his father Amaziah had done.
He set himself to seek God in the days of Zechariah, who taught him to fear God. And as long as he sought the LORD, God made him prosper.
Uzziah went out to wage war against the Philistines. He tore down the wall of Gath, the wall of Jabneh, and the wall of Ashdod, then built cities in the area of Ashdod and among the Philistines.
God helped him against the Philistines, against the Arabs who lived in Gur-baal, and against the Meunites.
The Ammonites brought tribute to Uzziah, and his fame spread far, to the border of Egypt, because he had become very strong.
Uzziah built towers in Jerusalem at the Corner Gate, at the Valley Gate, and at the angle of the wall, and fortified them.

He built towers in the wilderness and dug many cisterns, for he had much livestock both in the lowlands and on the plain. He had farmers and vinedressers in the hills and in the fertile fields, because he loved the soil.

Uzziah had an army ready for battle that went out to war by divisions, according to the number recorded by Jeiel the scribe and Maaseiah the officer, under the direction of Hananiah, one of the king’s officials.
The total number of the heads of families of the mighty warriors was 2,600.
Under their command was an army of 307,500 who made war with mighty strength, to help the king against the enemy.
Uzziah provided shields, spears, helmets, body armor, bows, and slingstones for the whole army.

In Jerusalem he made engines designed by skillful craftsmen, set on the towers and at the corners of the wall, to shoot arrows and hurl large stones. So his fame spread far, for he was marvelously helped until he became strong.

But when Uzziah had become strong, he grew proud and arrogant, which led to his own destruction. He was unfaithful to the LORD his God by entering the temple of the LORD to burn incense on the altar of incense.
Azariah the priest went in after him, together with eighty priests of the LORD who were men of courage.

They opposed King Uzziah and said, “O Uzziah, it is not your duty to burn incense to the LORD, but the duty of the priests, the sons of Aaron, who are consecrated to burn incense. Please leave the sanctuary, for you have been unfaithful, and this will bring you no honor from the LORD God.”
Then Uzziah was enraged. He was holding a censer to burn incense, and while he was angry with the priests in the house of the LORD beside the altar of incense, leprosy broke out on his forehead.

Azariah the chief priest and all the priests turned and looked at him, and behold, he was leprous on his forehead. So they quickly drove him out, and he himself also hurried to leave, because the LORD had struck him.

King Uzziah was a leper until the day of his death, and because he was a leper he lived in a separate house, for he was cut off from the house of the LORD. Meanwhile Jotham his son was over the royal household and governed the people of the land.
As for the rest of the acts of Uzziah, from beginning to end, Isaiah the prophet, the son of Amoz, recorded them.
Uzziah rested with his fathers, and they buried him near his fathers in the field of burial that belonged to the kings, for they said, “He is a leper.” And Jotham his son reigned in his place.
